verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: My brother Paulinian tells me that our friend has impugned certain things in my commentary on the Ephesians: some of these criticisms he committed to memory, and has indicated the actual passages impugned.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must not therefore refuse to meet his statements, and I beg the reader, if I am somewhat prolix in the statement and the refutation of his charges, to allow for the necessary conditions of the discussion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I am not accusing another but endeavouring to defend myself and to refute the false accusation of heresy which is thrown in my teeth.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: On the Epistle to the Ephesians Origen wrote three books.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Didymus and Apollinarius also composed works of their own.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: These I partly translated, partly adapted; my method is described in the following passage of my prologue: “This also I wish to state in my Preface.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Origen, you must know, wrote three books upon this Epistle, and I have partly followed him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Apollinarius also and Didymus published certain commentaries on it, from which I have culled some things, though but few; and, as seemed to me right, I put in or took out others; but I have done this in such a way that the careful reader may from the very first see how far the work is due to me, how far to others.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Whatever fault there is detected in the exposition given of this Epistle, if I am unable to shew that it exists in the Greek books from which I have stated it to have been translated into Latin, I will acknowledge that the fault is mine and not another’s.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: However, that I should not be thought to be raising quibbles, and by this artifice of self-excuse to be escaping from boldly meeting him, I will set out the actual passages which are adduced as evidences of my fault.
